author: @maxkennedy5073
text: Just a small word of warning, most drills, especially the inexpensive ones, do not have thrust bearings (meant to take sideways forces).  So as you said take very light cuts and don't do it too often.  You can actually replace the standard bearings for the drill with thrust bearings, not terribly hard and not particularly expensive.  Then you've got a nice combo machine.  But if you have something that already has them, like that lathe in the background or a router, use that.  Alternatively drill the end holes of a through slot and cut through in between with the circular saw.  I say this not as a critique but because as a young woodworker who couldn't afford much in the way of tools I ruined 2 drill presses doing too much stuff like this and trying to use the press as a vertical lathe.  If you don't have the right tool, replacing a drill press may be a bit of financial bother as well.  A more experienced chap taught me about the thrust bearings then I had 3 drill presses that worked.

author: @frankkrunk
text:
I highly recommend reading about Polhem, he should be the patron saint of men-in-sheds. He went from farmhand to ennobled by the king and elected to the royal academy of science, all because of his immense tinkering skills. 
His mechanical alphabet was a brilliant idea at the time. He called it "alphabet" because these basic mechanical principles are to an engineer what the alphabet is to a writer. Put them in the right order and you can do almost anything.

author: @Reman1975
text:
There's one potential issue with using a pillar drill this way. These drill presses either have the chucks on the spindle using a Jacobs taper stub, or a Morse taper recess. These are both pretty much just attached through a friction fit. Neither of them deal particularly well with side loading, and can drop off/out of the spindle when used like this. At best case it can ruin the work piece, but at worst you've suddenly got a chuck and sharp router bit doing the chicken dance on the machine table right where your fingers are.

For basic woodworking it's not a huge risk, but if you're doing this with hardwoods or soft metals the chances of the taper letting go rises considerably.

Yes, I know that there's going to be people reading this who've been doing this sort of thing for years without issue, but there's a lot of factors that raise or lower the chances of this happening, so it's best to know the potential risk.

author: @alanesq1
text:
Something to be aware of is that on a drill press the chuck is just held in by a taper so there is a risk that when applying side loads like this that it may spit the whole chuck out at you .   Having said that I have used mine to mill aluminium with some minor success.
BTW: If you search eBay for "milling table" there are some small ones for under 20ukp which if you spend some time adjusting the slides and most importantly add some arms to the hand wheels to effectively make them larger you make make yourself a poor mans milling machine.  I later built myself a mount for my router instead of using the drill press and this is better (and safer I think).
or search for "Cross Slide Drill Press Vise" and one of these vices for around £35 I have found really useful on a drill press.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ianturnbull985 
"Yes, why not just use a crank for that?"
Because a crank requires you to stand there and turn it.
A motor allows you to turn it on and walk away once you have verified that everything is running properly.

author: @eclsnowman
text: Great video. Love the mechanism. I'm certainly no Nervous Nelly. I'll just mention that because of the way drill chucks fit into a drill press with a morse taper, side loading them can make them want to come flying out. Since you were using a straight flute bit it's not so bad. But if you've got a router bit that has a spiral flute like an end mill, then apply side load it'll have a high tendency to want to pull itself out with exciting effects. Ask me how I know :-)

replies.author: @johnwilson3668
replies.text:
Hi if your drill has a tube spindle you can cut off the ejection tang  and drill and tap the end of the taper and fit a draw bar (screwed rod ) . the taper can be ejected by 
undoing the nut on the top and tapping the screwed rod The tapers are not usualy hardened . if it is heat the area of the taper you are going to alter until red hot then let it 
cool down naturally . this will soften the steel (naturalisation) .This  procedure will secure the cutter

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I can imagine how you know mate - happened to me too - but like I said in the vid - if you don't know what you are doing here - don't do it lol - still - awesome point and I really like John's add on - to be honest mate it is part of the reason I do vids like this - I can rely on the comments being good and helpful - the one thing I hate is when people say oh no don't do that - if folks say do that but also be aware of this and do this to help - well that's just awesome

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very nice mate - thank you for the great tip

replies.author: @johnwilson3668
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Hi Robert There is another way of holding in a taper if there is no hole through the spindle. Drill and tap a hole in the side of the tang 
(only half way through so in does not weaken it) the bottom of the tapped hole must line up with the bottom of the ejection slot when tapped in place. when you screw 
in a grub screw tight it will ightly push on the bottom of the drill ejection slot . I went through all this many years ago holding a 6 inch dia drill  in a radial arm drill . You can
imagine how frightened the drill operator was with a 30 horse power motor driving the 6 inch drill which kept falling out of the taper

author: @johnwilson3668
text:
Great video . I have used that method myself in the past .There is another cool thing you can  do ,is by bolting a sacrificial plywood table to the iron table and  drilling a peg hole say
300mm from the cutter diameter  .Then place the material you want to cut on the peg, bring down the cutter then rotate the piece of material on the peg and you have got a 600mm diameter 
disc .This will work on any material the router cutter can cut eg wood plastic When I have done this procedure I  cut out a square .The dia I want plus the dia of the cutter x 2  and a few millimetres
  extra and cut of the diagonal corners so they do not hit the support tube of the drill and obviously the peg hole in the centre

Nice hack Rob, as others have said it will shorten the life of the bearings but if done very occasionally in soft materials then it shouldn't be too much of an issue, 
I use a slitting saw with my pillar drill for through slotting, you might want to give that a go,

A cross/compound vise helps out and reduces tool chatter significantly, I picked up a used one for £15 a couple of years ago, very handy

author: @paulgw101
text: Hi Robert, Just a quick observation. The direction of machining should be the opposite direction when working with a fence behind. Push the workpiece right to left, otherwise the workpiece will bind & snatch. Push into the closest cutting edge to the fence.

author: @billbaggins
text:
Sorry Rob but NOOOOO this is a terrible idea. I'm all for making do with what you have and a bit of adaptation. The danger factor does increase a bit but the main problem will be the premature death of your drill press, they are only designed for vertical drilling. The bearings in the quill are not designed for a lateral load and will develop significant runout and fail very quickly. Best way to make slots or cut an internal shape is with a jigsaw in conjunction with a fence for accuracy or a router. Reasonable quality routers are not always cheap but jigsaws are very cheap.
If it'a one off job your method would work but would be better done as a series of drilled holes cleaned up with a rasp or using very light pressure and multiple passes with your method.
Many drill presses have been destroyed by using them as a milling machine.

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
Premature bearing failure will be an issue if you do a lot of routing with a pillar drill,
If you only hack out a few shapes per year then it shouldn't be a problem, especially in wood or soft plastic, trying this with metal will cause bearing issues though, 

If you have the funds for a router table, milling machine and lathe then that's the way to go.... Otherwise a pillar drill, compound slide vise and a bit of head scratching will get  you through most light duty jobs

replies.author: @billbaggins
replies.text: @sofa-lofa4241 Yep you can do a lot with basic tools on basic jobs but all tools have a limit. I got sick of lines of holes and hand filing and lashed out on a small mill, so much nicer and easier.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I appreciate the advice mate - especially for others - but i cut slots rarely in soft materials this way and have been doing it in this old drill for something like 8 years now - i suspect this is one of those things that just depends

replies.author: @billbaggins
replies.text: Cheers mate, yep was really advice for others. Many are on tight budgets so getting maximum life from a tool is important. You can't drink beer and play with power tools if the tool is broken...you just end up drunk 😁
